title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: csdm package implements econometric methods panel data cross-sectional dependence (CSD). many applications, observations across units (e.g., countries, firms, regions) independent—macroeconomic shocks, trade relationships, spillovers create correlation across cross-sectional units. csdm package provides robust estimators account dependence structure, plus diagnostic tests detect characterize . vignette demonstrates four core estimation methods related inference tools real panel data Penn World Table (PWT).

title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence

Let =1,…,Ni = 1, \ldots, N index cross-sectional units t=1,…,Tt = 1, \ldots, T index time. baseline heterogeneous panel model yit=αi+βi′xit+uit,=1,…,N,t=1,…,T y_{} = \alpha_i + \beta_i' x_{} + u_{}, \qquad = 1, \ldots, N,\; t = 1, \ldots, T : yity_{} outcome variable unit ii time tt αi\alpha_i unit-specific intercept βi\beta_i (k×1)(k \times 1) vector unit-specific slopes xitx_{} (k×1)(k \times 1) vector explanatory variables uitu_{} error term, may exhibit cross-sectional dependence inner product βi′xit\beta_i' x_{} scalar-valued. Heterogeneous slopes allow unit respond differently regressors. many applications, cross-sectional dependence arises error term contains unobserved common factors. estimators implemented csdm() differ handle dependence whether allow dynamic adjustment.

title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: Mean Group estimator fits separate regressions unit averages resulting coefficients: β̂MG=1N∑=1Nβ̂\hat{\beta}_{MG} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{=1}^N \hat{\beta}_i Key idea: Estimation performed unit unit, pooling slope coefficients across cross-sectional units. Interpretation: β̂MG\hat{\beta}_{MG} cross-sectional average unit-specific estimates slope coefficients allowed differ across units Properties: accommodates slope heterogeneity requires sufficient time-series information within unit explicitly model cross-sectional dependence Use case: natural benchmark main concern heterogeneous slopes explicit factor structure imposed.

title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: CCE estimator augments unit regression cross-sectional averages proxy unobserved common factors: yit=αi+βi′xit+γi′z‾t+vit y_{} = \alpha_i + \beta_i' x_{} + \gamma_i' \bar{z}_t + v_{} z‾t\bar{z}_t collects cross-sectional averages specified csdm_csa(), example z‾t=(y‾t,x‾t),x‾t=1N∑=1Nxit,y‾t=1N∑=1Nyit. \bar{z}_t = (\bar{y}_t, \bar{x}_t), \qquad \bar{x}_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{=1}^N x_{}, \qquad \bar{y}_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{=1}^N y_{}. Key idea: Cross-sectional averages serve proxies latent common factors induce dependence across units. Interpretation: βi\beta_i measures unit-specific effect conditional included cross-sectional averages γi\gamma_i captures unit-specific exposure common components z‾t\bar{z}_t proxy. Properties: allows heterogeneous slopes augments regression cross-sectional averages supplied csa suitable cross-sectional dependence driven latent common shocks Use case: dependence across units believed reflect common unobserved factors.

title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: DCCE estimator extends CCE dynamic settings including lagged dependent variables, optional distributed lags regressors, lagged cross-sectional averages: yit=αi+∑p=1Pϕipyi,t−p+∑q=0Qβiq′xi,t−q+∑s=0Sδis′z‾t−s+eit y_{} = \alpha_i + \sum_{p=1}^{P} \phi_{ip} y_{,t-p} + \sum_{q=0}^{Q} \beta_{iq}' x_{,t-q} + \sum_{s=0}^{S} \delta_{}' \bar{z}_{t-s} + e_{} dynamic structure controlled csdm_lr() cross-sectional averages lags controlled csdm_csa(). Key idea: Dynamics introduced directly unit equation, lagged cross-sectional averages help absorb common factor dependence time. Interpretation: ϕip\phi_{ip} captures unit-specific persistence βiq\beta_{iq} captures contemporaneous lagged effects regressors δis\delta_{} captures effect contemporaneous lagged common components Properties: allows heterogeneous dynamic adjustment across units combines lagged dependent variables, optional distributed lags, cross-sectional augmentation requires enough time periods support chosen lag structure Use case: outcome persistent time cross-sectional dependence remains important.

title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: current csdm() implementation, model = "cs_ardl" obtained first estimating cross-sectionally augmented ARDL-style regression levels, using dynamic specification model = "dcce", transforming estimated unit-specific coefficients adjustment long-run parameters. underlying unit-level regression yit=αi+∑p=1Pϕipyi,t−p+∑q=0Qβiq′xi,t−q+∑s=0Sωis′z‾t−s+eit y_{} = \alpha_i + \sum_{p=1}^{P} \phi_{ip} y_{,t-p} + \sum_{q=0}^{Q} \beta_{iq}' x_{,t-q} + \sum_{s=0}^{S} \omega_{}' \bar{z}_{t-s} + e_{} dynamic specification, implied error-correction form Δyit=αi+φi(yi,t−1−θi′xi,t−1)+∑j=1P−1λijΔyi,t−j+∑j=0Q−1ψij′Δxi,t−j+∑s=0Sω̃′z‾t−s+eit \Delta y_{} = \alpha_i + \varphi_i \left( y_{,t-1} - \theta_i' x_{,t-1} \right) + \sum_{j=1}^{P-1} \lambda_{ij} \Delta y_{,t-j} + \sum_{j=0}^{Q-1} \psi_{ij}' \Delta x_{,t-j} + \sum_{s=0}^{S} \tilde{\omega}_{}' \bar{z}_{t-s} + e_{} dynamic structure controlled csdm_lr() cross-sectional averages supplied csdm_csa(). Key idea: cs_ardl reports implied short-run long-run quantities cross-sectionally augmented ARDL fit. Interpretation: θi\theta_i unit-specific long-run relationship φi\varphi_i implied speed adjustment back toward equilibrium ψij\psi_{ij} captures short-run effects changes regressors ω̃\tilde{\omega}_{} captures role common cross-sectional components Properties: supports heterogeneous short-run long-run dynamics combines ARDL-style dynamics cross-sectional augmentation recovers adjustment long-run coefficients estimated lag polynomials rather fitting separate ECM directly Use case: objective study long-run relationships together heterogeneous short-run adjustment panels affected common factors.

title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: PWT_60_07 dataset contains macroeconomic indicators 93 countries covering 1960–2007 (48 years). Key variables include: id: Country identifier year: Calendar year (1960–2007) log_rgdpo: Log real GDP per capita log_hc: Log human capital index log_ck: Log capital stock log_ngd: Log government debt (control variable) panel relatively balanced. use growth regressions: modeling log-GDP (log_rgdpo) function human capital (log_hc), capital stock (log_ck), government debt (log_ngd), test cross-sectional dependence residuals.

title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: Interpretation: MG estimate suggests average across countries, increases human capital, capital stock, changes debt associated changes real GDP. standard errors reflect cross-country heterogeneity relationships.
code: # MG: Separate regression per country, then average coefficients fit_mg <- csdm(   log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd,   data = df,   id = "id",    time = "year",   model = "mg" )  print(fit_mg) #> csdm fit (mg) #> Formula: log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd #> N: 15, T: 38 #>             Estimate Std.Error #> (Intercept)   6.5609    0.8648 #> log_hc        0.1725    0.8530 #> log_ck        0.3056    0.1359 #> log_ngd       0.7800    0.3777 summary(fit_mg) #> csdm summary: Mean Group Model (MG) #> Formula: log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd #> N: 15, T: 38 #> Number of obs: 570 #> R-squared (mg): 0.9449 #> CD = 3.2379, p = 0.0012 #> (For additional CD diagnostics, use cd_test()) #>  #> Mean Group: #>              Coef. title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: Interpretation: accounting global shocks (via cross-sectional averages variables), CCE coefficients standard errors may differ MG. indicates whether common factors (e.g., technology, energy prices) major source cross-sectional dependence.
code: # CCE: Add cross-sectional means to control for common shocks fit_cce <- csdm(   log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd,   data = df,   id = "id",    time = "year",   model = "cce",   csa = csdm_csa(vars = c("log_rgdpo", "log_hc", "log_ck", "log_ngd")) )  print(fit_cce) #> csdm fit (cce) #> Formula: log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd #> N: 15, T: 38 #>             Estimate Std.Error #> (Intercept)   1.9003    2.1195 #> log_hc       -1.4921    1.0152 #> log_ck        0.1367    0.0956 #> log_ngd       0.8075    0.2972 summary(fit_cce) #> csdm summary: Static Common Correlated Error Model (CCE) #> Formula: log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd #> N: 15, T: 38 #> Number of obs: 570 #> R-squared (mg): 0.9777 #> CD = -2.6758, p = 0.0075 #> (For additional CD diagnostics, use cd_test()) #>  #> Mean Group: #>               Coef. title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: Interpretation: DCCE model includes lagged GDP capture dynamic adjustment. lagged coefficient typically lies 0.8–0.95, indicating strong income persistence. coefficients variables represent short-run elasticities; compute long-run effects, divide (1−lag coefficient)(1 - \text{lag coefficient}).
code: # DCCE: Include dynamics and cross-sectional means # Use lagged dependent variable to capture dynamic adjustment fit_dcce <- csdm(   log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd,   data = df,   id = "id",    time = "year",   model = "dcce",   csa = csdm_csa(     vars = c("log_rgdpo", "log_hc", "log_ck", "log_ngd"),      lags = 3   ),   lr = csdm_lr(type = "ardl", ylags = 1, xdlags = 0) )  print(fit_dcce) #> csdm fit (dcce) #> Formula: log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd #> N: 15, T: 38 #>                Estimate Std.Error #> (Intercept)      9.2888    8.0386 #> log_hc          -1.9558    1.5659 #> log_ck           0.6666    0.2424 #> log_ngd         -0.3178    1.1271 #> lag1_log_rgdpo  -0.0745    0.0555 summary(fit_dcce) #> csdm summary: Dynamic Common Correlated Error Model (DCCE) #> Formula: log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd #> N: 15, T: 38 #> Number of obs: 525 #> R-squared (mg): 0.9861 #> CD = -2.392, p = 0.0168 #> (For additional CD diagnostics, use cd_test()) #>  #> Mean Group: #>                  Coef. Std. Err.       z  P>|z| Signif. CI 2.5% CI 97.5% #> (Intercept)     9.2888    8.0386  1.1555 0.2479         -6.4666  25.0441 #> log_hc         -1.9558    1.5659 -1.2490 0.2117         -5.0249   1.1132 #> log_ck          0.6666    0.2424  2.7499 0.0060      **  0.1915   1.1417 #> log_ngd        -0.3178    1.1271 -0.2819 0.7780         -2.5268   1.8913 #> lag1_log_rgdpo -0.0745    0.0555 -1.3424 0.1795         -0.1834   0.0343 #>  #> Mean Group Variables: log_hc, log_ck, log_ngd, lag1_log_rgdpo #> Cross Sectional Averaged Variables: log_rgdpo, log_hc, log_ck, log_ngd (lags=3) #>  #> Signif. codes:  0 '***' 0.001 '**' 0.01 '*' 0.05 '.' 0.1 ' ' 1

title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: Interpretation: CS-ARDL model returns short-run coefficients (immediate response shocks) long-run coefficients (equilibrium effect full adjustment). long-run elasticities often larger short-run responses, consistent gradual accumulation effects capital human capital.

text: Pesaran CD statistic : CD=2N(N−1)∑=1N−1∑j=+1Nρ̂ijTCD = \sqrt{\frac{2}{N(N-1)}} \sum_{=1}^{N-1} \sum_{j=+1}^{N} \hat{\rho}_{ij} \sqrt{T} ρ̂ij\hat{\rho}_{ij} cross-sectional correlation residuals units ii jj. test statistic approximately standard normal null. Interpretation: Large |CD||CD| rejects independence; positive negative correlations flagged. general CD test works even NN fixed T→∞T \\infty.

what: Running CD Tests with Seed Selection
title: Introduction to csdm: Panel Data Models with Cross-Sectional Dependence
text: cd_test() function accepts fitted model computes test variants. Tests use random seed initialize pseudo-random computations (cdw cdw+); setting seed ensures reproducibility numerical results across runs. Interpreting Results: CD statistic p-value < 0.05: Reject null CSD independence; residuals correlated across units. CDw, CDw+, CD* variants: Provide robustness checks; reject null, CSD strongly evidenced. Magnitude: Large |CD||CD| statistics (e.g., |CD|>3|CD| > 3) indicate substantial economically meaningful dependence. practice, models account cross-sectional dependence (like MG without augmentation) typically show significant CD test rejections, justifying use CSD-robust methods like CCE DCCE.
code: # Test MG residuals for CSD cd_mg <- cd_test(fit_mg, type = "CD") print(cd_mg) #> Cross-sectional dependence tests #> N = 15, T = 38 #>  #>    statistic p.value #> CD     3.238   0.001  # Test CCE residuals for CSD set.seed(1234) cd_cce <- cd_test(fit_cce, type = "all") print(cd_cce) #> Cross-sectional dependence tests #> N = 15, T = 38 #>  #>        statistic p.value #> CD        -2.676   0.007 #> CDw       -1.532   0.126 #> CDw+     150.431   0.000 #> CDstar    -2.835   0.005  # Test DCCE residuals for CSD set.seed(1234) cd_dcce <- cd_test(fit_dcce, type = "CDw") print(cd_dcce) #> Cross-sectional dependence tests #> N = 15, T = 35 #>  #>     statistic p.value #> CDw     1.092   0.275  # Test CS-ARDL residuals for CSD set.seed(1234) cd_csardl <- cd_test(fit_csardl, type = "all") print(cd_csardl) #> Cross-sectional dependence tests #> N = 15, T = 35 #>  #>        statistic p.value #> CD        -2.392   0.017 #> CDw        1.092   0.275 #> CDw+     130.969   0.000 #> CDstar    -1.769   0.077

text: current csdm() implementation, model = "cs_ardl" obtained first estimating cross-sectionally augmented ARDL-style regression levels, using dynamic specification model = "dcce", transforming estimated unit-specific coefficients adjustment long-run parameters. underlying unit-level regression yit=αi+∑p=1Pϕipyi,t−p+∑q=0Qβiq′xi,t−q+∑s=0Sωis′z‾t−s+eit y_{} = \alpha_i + \sum_{p=1}^{P} \phi_{ip} y_{,t-p} + \sum_{q=0}^{Q} \beta_{iq}' x_{,t-q} + \sum_{s=0}^{S} \omega_{}' \bar{z}_{t-s} + e_{} dynamic specification, implied error-correction form Δyit=αi+φi(yi,t−1−θi′xi,t−1)+∑j=1P−1λijΔyi,t−j+∑j=0Q−1ψij′Δxi,t−j+∑s=0Sω̃′z‾t−s+eit \Delta y_{} = \alpha_i + \varphi_i \left( y_{,t-1} - \theta_i' x_{,t-1} \right) + \sum_{j=1}^{P-1} \lambda_{ij} \Delta y_{,t-j} + \sum_{j=0}^{Q-1} \psi_{ij}' \Delta x_{,t-j} + \sum_{s=0}^{S} \tilde{\omega}_{}' \bar{z}_{t-s} + e_{} dynamic structure controlled csdm_lr() cross-sectional averages supplied csdm_csa(). Key idea: cs_ardl reports implied short-run long-run quantities cross-sectionally augmented ARDL fit. Interpretation: θi\theta_i unit-specific long-run relationship φi\varphi_i implied speed adjustment back toward equilibrium ψij\psi_{ij} captures short-run effects changes regressors ω̃\tilde{\omega}_{} captures role common cross-sectional components Properties: supports heterogeneous short-run long-run dynamics combines ARDL-style dynamics cross-sectional augmentation recovers adjustment long-run coefficients estimated lag polynomials rather fitting separate ECM directly Use case: objective study long-run relationships together heterogeneous short-run adjustment panels affected common factors.

text: cd_test() function accepts fitted model computes test variants. Tests use random seed initialize pseudo-random computations (cdw cdw+); setting seed ensures reproducibility numerical results across runs. CDw, CDw+, CD* variants: Provide robustness checks; reject null, CSD strongly evidenced. Magnitude: Large |CD||CD| statistics (e.g., |CD|>3|CD| > 3) indicate substantial economically meaningful dependence. practice, models account cross-sectional dependence (like MG without augmentation) typically show significant CD test rejections, justifying use CSD-robust methods like CCE DCCE.

title: Panel Model Estimation with Cross-Sectional Dependence — csdm
text: Let \(= 1, \ldots, N\) index cross-sectional units \(t = 1, \ldots, T\) index time. baseline heterogeneous panel model $$y_{} = \alpha_i + \beta_i^T x_{} + u_{}.$$ \(\alpha_i\) unit-specific intercept, \(x_{}\) vector regressors, \(\beta_i\) vector unit-specific slopes, \(u_{}\) error term may exhibit cross-sectional dependence. Cross-sectional averages specified csdm_csa() dynamic long-run structure specified csdm_lr(). keeps model interface consistent across estimators allowing degree cross-sectional augmentation lag structure vary application. Implemented estimators MG (Pesaran Smith, 1995) Mean Group estimator fits separate regressions unit averages resulting coefficients: $$\hat{\beta}_{MG} = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{=1}^N \hat{\beta}_i.$$ estimator accommodates slope heterogeneity explicitly model cross-sectional dependence. CCE (Pesaran, 2006) Regressions augmented cross-sectional averages proxy unobserved common factors: $$y_{} = \alpha_i + \beta_i^T x_{} + \gamma_i^T \bar{z}_{t} + v_{}.$$ common choice $$\bar{z}_t = (\bar{y}_t, \bar{x}_t),$$ $$\bar{x}_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{=1}^N x_{}, \qquad \bar{y}_t = \frac{1}{N}\sum_{=1}^N y_{}.$$ generally, \(\bar{z}_t\) collects cross-sectional averages specified csa. DCCE (Chudik Pesaran, 2015) Dynamic CCE extends CCE allowing lagged dependent variables lagged cross-sectional averages: $$y_{} = \alpha_i + \sum_{p=1}^{P} \phi_{ip} y_{,t-p} + \beta_i^T x_{} + \sum_{q=0}^{Q} \delta_{iq}^T \bar{z}_{t-q} + e_{}.$$ package implementation, lagged dependent variables distributed lags regressors controlled lr, contemporaneous lagged cross-sectional averages controlled csa. CS-ARDL (Chudik Pesaran, 2015) package implementation, model = "cs_ardl" obtained first estimating cross-sectionally augmented ARDL-style regression levels, using dynamic specification model = "dcce", transforming unit-specific coefficients adjustment long-run parameters. underlying unit-level regression form $$y_{} = \alpha_i + \sum_{p=1}^{P} \phi_{ip} y_{,t-p} + \sum_{q=0}^{Q} \beta_{iq}^T x_{,t-q} + \sum_{s=0}^{S} \omega_{}^T \bar{z}_{t-s} + e_{}.$$ dynamic specification, package recovers implied error-correction form $$\Delta y_{} = \alpha_i + \varphi_i \left(y_{,t-1} - \theta_i^T x_{,t-1}\right) + \sum_{j=1}^{P-1} \lambda_{ij} \Delta y_{,t-j} + \sum_{j=0}^{Q-1} \psi_{ij}^T \Delta x_{,t-j} + \sum_{s=0}^{S} \tilde{\omega}_{}^T \bar{z}_{t-s} + e_{},$$ \(\varphi_i\) adjustment coefficient \(\theta_i\) implied long-run relationship. current implementation, quantities computed estimated lag polynomials rather direct ECM regression. Identification assumptions MG requires sufficient time-series variation within unit. CCE relies cross-sectional averages acting proxies latent common factors, together adequate cross-sectional time dimensions. DCCE additionally requires enough time periods support lagged dependent variables, distributed lags, lagged cross-sectional averages. CS-ARDL requires sufficient time length distributed-lag structure intended applications short-run dynamics long-run relationships interest presence common factors.

what: Extract residual matrices for panel diagnostics — get_residuals
title: Extract residual matrices for panel diagnostics — get_residuals
text: Unified accessor returns \(N x T\) residual matrix suitable cross-sectional dependence diagnostics post-estimation analysis.

title: Extract residual matrices for panel diagnostics — get_residuals
code: get_residuals(object, type = c("auto", "cce", "pca", "pca_std"), strict = TRUE)

title: Extract residual matrices for panel diagnostics — get_residuals
text: object fitted model object supported package (e.g., class csdm_fit), directly numeric matrix residuals shaped \(N x T\). type Character string selecting residuals return available: one "auto", "cce", "pca", "pca_std". "auto": prefer standardized PCA residuals present, otherwise PCA residuals, otherwise CCE residuals, otherwise object matrix. "cce": residuals CCE-augmented per-unit regressions. "pca": residuals removing estimated factors \(\hat v_{}\). "pca_std": "pca" residuals standardized unit-specific scale (recommended CD). strict Logical; TRUE, error unsupported objects. FALSE, return NULL residuals found.

title: Extract residual matrices for panel diagnostics — get_residuals
text: numeric matrix residuals rows = units columns = time, preserving rownames colnames available; NULL nothing suitable found strict = FALSE.

title: Extract residual matrices for panel diagnostics — get_residuals
text: cce Residuals cross-sectionally augmented unit regressions. pca Residuals principal-component factor removal. pca_std PCA residuals standardized unit-specific scale. auto Priority rule: pca_std -> pca -> cce -> generic residual slots.

title: Extract residual matrices for panel diagnostics — get_residuals
text: returned matrix intended diagnostics operate unit-time panels, including cd_test(). Missing values preserved unless downstream routines explicitly filter balance panel.

title: Extract residual matrices for panel diagnostics — get_residuals
code: data(PWT_60_07, package = "csdm") df <- PWT_60_07 ids <- unique(df$id)[1:10] df_small <- df[df$id %in% ids & df$year >= 1970, ]  fit <- csdm(   log_rgdpo ~ log_hc + log_ck + log_ngd,   data = df_small,   id = "id",   time = "year",   model = "cce",   csa = csdm_csa(vars = c("log_rgdpo", "log_hc", "log_ck", "log_ngd")) )  E <- get_residuals(fit, type = "auto") dim(E) #> [1] 10 38

title: Variance-covariance of Mean-Group (MG) averages — pooled_vcov
text: Computes covariance matrix MG estimator \(\bar{\beta} = N^{-1} \sum_i \hat\beta_i\), using cross-sectional covariance unit-specific slopes dividing effective sample sizes per coefficient (handles missing entries per unit).

title: Variance-covariance of Mean-Group (MG) averages — pooled_vcov
code: pooled_vcov(beta_i, weights = NULL, pairwise = TRUE)

title: Variance-covariance of Mean-Group (MG) averages — pooled_vcov
text: beta_i Numeric matrix unit-specific coefficients (\(N x K\)); rows = units, columns = coefficients. May contain NAs. weights Optional numeric vector length N nonnegative weights summing 1. NULL, uses equal weights. pairwise Logical; use pairwise-complete covariances across units (default TRUE).

title: Variance-covariance of Mean-Group (MG) averages — pooled_vcov
text: K x K covariance matrix MG mean, dimnames inherited colnames(beta_i).

title: Variance-covariance of Mean-Group (MG) averages — pooled_vcov
text: equal weights, diagonal entries coincide \(\widehat{\mathrm{Var}}(\hat\beta_{ij}) / N_{\text{eff},j}\). -diagonals scaled analogously using pairwise effective N. weights provided, computes \(\mathrm{Var}(\sum_i w_i \hat\beta_i)\) using weighted covariance.
